speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two-- the survivor is Samuel Friedman. What did your family do on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday night, we had a Friday night meal. My mother blessed the candles. And we had wine. We said the Berachot. And we had a meal. That's all. Saturday, usually we didn't go to the shul. But from time to time, we did, especially on the holidays. Every holiday, we went to the shul The shul was-- we called it the German shul. But there was an orthodox one, and so many, many other small places of worship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe one occasion of you going to the shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's not very much to describe. We-- I went with my father. We sat together. And I had to read and follow the service, read in the Seder-- you know what I mean-- and follow the service. And then I tried-- it depends on my age, of course. When I was 11 or 12, I tried to escape as soon as possible. Later on, I maybe I sat a bit longer. In the gardens of the synagogue, we played with all other youngsters. You know, the band of children were running and shouting and going and coming. This was the usual way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bar mitzvah, I had at home. My two grandfathers somehow didn't agree, one with the other. And they didn't come to the bar mitzvah. But my uncle, my mother's elder brother, who was orthodox, he came. And he was the one who presided about the things. To prepare myself to the bar mitzvah, it was a hired orthodox man who taught me my haftarah and who wrote my speech in Hebrew. And I started my speech in Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means, I am welcoming the day when I was celebrating the bar mitzvah when I was 13 years old. This is the beginning of the speech. My haftarah was from Amos, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and so on, and so on, so on, so on. Yes, my friends didn't come to my bar mitzvah because they were only late invited. And it was some, some, some misunderstanding. And no one from my friends-- only the family was there. For my bar mitzvah I remember I brought from somewhere a [INAUDIBLE], which you call today disc player. And then I-- first time, I heard the music of Ketelbey, 'In the Chinese Garden,' and the 'Persian Market' of Ketelbey. And what else-- and some other of the very well-known operas. I had maybe a dozen, dozen records with the tube. And I was listening to the music. And then I probably-- I had it for a longer time or so. But still, I was first time introduced to this music. Because we didn't have a radio, not speaking about television.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get your radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first radio I got when I was 16 after a long, long talk with my father. We bought a secondhand radio, old fashioned Phillips, this shape. And I was operating this. So I-- long wires to my bed. And then I knew how to find the short waves, and the medium, and so on. And I, I enjoyed the radio very, very much, especially during the Olympics. During the Olympics in '36, in Germany, every day it was a communique about what happened, and Jesse Owens-- and you know Jesse Owens-- and so on, and so on. So the radio was very, very useful. This is the first time I had a radio. And well, I was using it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some other programs you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they are typical Polish programs, comedy things and-- which I, I don't, I don't recall very well. But still, what I was interested-- I was listening [? to ?] [? music, ?] as I told you before, from Bucharest. I remember even the signal from Bucharest, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And then from Budapest, eight o'clock in the evening, Budapest was absolutely magnificent gypsy music. And during the day, I was at school, of course. But in evenings I was listening to the radio-- communiques, news, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sports, if any, did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't play sports. I would say that the only sport was-- if you can call it sport-- was swimming in the river and rowing the boat, just as a recreation on Saturdays or Sundays. But we didn't play any sport. At school, we played a bit of soccer in the backyard. But this is very hard to say sport. But any sport, sporting activities, were sport lessons at school. We had the gymnasium, they called it-- a large room with all-- with some facilities-- climbing, and rowing, and running, and, and, and things like this. This was the sport activities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about business activities in your hometown? Who was running the business, and tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wouldn't know who is running the business. Because I was not involved. I wasn't interested. But I know that there was a lot of shopkeepers in town who were earning their money from the business of selling and buying. And then there were wholesalers and manufacturers. In our town was a large textile manufacturing business or businesses. And I know that they were doing well.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I know. But still, I, I wouldn't know. I was a bit too young to even ask about business. Who cares about? Was it in Polish or in Jewish hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a question. Of course, the Jews were very, very active in business. They had better education. And their arithmetics was much better, and in head without calculators, which makes-- improves the business activities. You see, in-- when where the Jews came and they started any business, they were much better equipped to, to run the business than other people. Other people said, I'm going to a factory to earn my money. This is the first thing. Later on, maybe open a [? mill ?] or something. The Jews were much better equipped to do it. You know the calculation. You know your cost. You know your-- so I can imagine that the Jews were very, very active in our town in business. And the larger factory, as far as I know, they were in Jewish hands and in German hands-- definitely in German hands as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of some of the factories and big businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fibiger was the piano factory, very well known, and very old, on my street. I was living there. Then it was [? Hishervick ?] and [? Apostrick. ?] This was my uncle, very large wholesaler of textile and very, very rich people. Miller Textile Mill-- and the others, I wouldn't know. I wouldn't remember the others. There are many, but Polish names of some kind. I can't remember them. I wasn't interested in this. I wasn't involved at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the local markets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a local market where people came from all over the place. I can't remember if this was once a week or twice a week. I wasn't, again, involved in the market. But I know there is market. And people came, especially the farmers, the Polish farmers. They brought butter, and eggs, and meat, and whatever, and, of course, other things like textiles and, and haberdashery. The market was large-- not organized, but still, you could get things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the scenes in the market, the smells, the sounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know, because I-- if I came to the market to pick up something or to buy something with mother, or with my cousin later on when-- not, not very much interested in. A lot of people, a lot of noise, a lot of smells, but to describe them really is very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you graduate from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew graduation was in March, '38, exactly when the Germans entered Austria, the Anschluss with Austria. This was the matricula-- the exams in Hebrew for matriculation was in March. And the Polish, the last matriculation, was in May. The matriculation in Polish was divided into in writing and in oral. In writing, we went to a Polish high school, not in our high school, where the Polish director and the Polish headmaster was supervising. And we were sitting, each one, at a table. And we had four days of examination. The subjects were mathematics, Polish literature, history, and the fourth was-- I can't remember-- history, Polish, mathematics, science. From each one, we had to get a mark. And if the marks were good enough, we were allowed to go to the oral examination. The oral examination was four, five weeks later. And it was at our school-- a committee of, I think, 11 teachers and a representative of the Polish government, not to let the Jews to go so easy. And this was the oral one. I was admitted to the oral. Four of our fellow students were not. So, in a class of 20, 16 went to the oral examinations. And my girlfriend failed, too. And I had to tell her about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in an afternoon-- every afternoon, every morning and afternoon, they had two people, two students for examination. I was in the afternoon with my friend of mine. And we came to school. And we were asked questions in mathematics, in geography, everything-- 11 subjects.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, we were waiting outside. And then came the [? shamash, ?] as I mentioned before, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], And he called, both of you. Now, this sign, both of you, meant that we passed. So usually, they called only the one who passed. And later on, we got the certificates, and which I have still with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about relations between Jew and non-Jews in your hometown before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the anti-Semit-- anti-Semitism was growing and growing. We have the feeling that it's getting worse and worse from the newspaper, from the Polish newspapers, and from the, and from the signs put on, in the street-- 'Kalisz without Jews,' or 'Don't buy at Jewish shops,' and so on, and so on. It was growing all the time, all the time. And from time to time, some-- one of us was beaten. One of our student was beaten by the Polish [? sacks. ?] And the situation wasn't very pleasant. And the papers were-- the whole, the politics of the Polish government was slowly, after the death of the Marshal Pilsudski, was changing, and changing against the Jews. In the Senate and the Sejm, which is the Parliament, were voices against the Jews, against the ritual, ritual slaughter of for kosher beef, and some other things. And all the time was something against the Jews. So, the relationship, you know, wasn't good and wasn't pleasant. And you had the feeling you are not wanted. And you don't, don't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about those posters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the poster was-- which I remember so well-- was 'Kalisz without the Jews.' This is the one big one was always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like it? How did it look?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I remember white and red. I think white was-- red letters on a white background. This is what I remember, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very large between-- on the street, between one side of the street and the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who posted it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the Endecja. [? ND, ?] it means in Polish Narodowa Demokracja. National Democracy, they called themselves. The ND was the party-- this is the anti-Semitic party which was against the Jews. And they, and they started the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the police react to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never asked police about-- they wouldn't, they wouldn't react about this. No Jews was in the police force.

speaker: interviewer
text: The police didn't take it down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't take it down. Because it was always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after your graduation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my graduation, we had-- as a law, in Poland, every graduate has to go to a police, to a military officers school. The officers school was exclusive for Poles. And the Jewish graduates-- I have a picture of the graduates standing and waiting for the commission-- and the Jewish graduates were granted a year leave to go for studies, or to go away, or to whatever to do. But they were not admitted to the poli-- the military officers corps to study and to graduate. This was-- I speak about '38. Before the-- the years before, some of the Jews went to the poli-- to the military officers school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were granted a year's leave. And we could go study. So I started in-- I enrolled at the University of Granowo, Polytechnic of Granowo, to study engineering. And I was accepted. And I had to prepare my papers. And eventually, late in the year '38, I went to, to France, traveling by train through Germany-- [LAUGHS] no incidents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I arrived in France. And I, I didn't know French, because my language, my foreign language was German. And I started French quickly to, to be-- and the first year went by studying at the university and struggling with French. And in my group was one [? Primo ?] [? Levi, ?] who came with a group of Italian students from Italy, from Trieste. From-- not from Trieste, from the town of [? Yatra. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Turin?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Turino, ?] [? Turino. ?] They came from [? Turino, ?] the group. And I was in the group as a, as a foreigner. And so we started mathematics, and physics, and other things at the university-- not very successfully, because my French was nonexistent. And the first year went by, so I could later on continue. But I couldn't continue because of the war. In the meantime, I had some knowledge of French, which was very useful later on. I could communicate in French. The year ended in June. I went to Paris. And I spent two weeks in Paris visiting all the places which I wanted to know. I-- and then I met my friend, [? Mietek ?] [? Colton, ?] who was studying in Pari. And we made the journey back to Poland together. When we arrived at the, into the, at the border town, [? Kale ?] [? Grente, ?] near Strausberg, the Germans were controlling our passports. And they took the-- mine passport was somehow good, because in Polish, I wasn't, I wasn't Samuel. I was [? Schmole. ?] And they couldn't-- they didn't know what is this [? Schmole. ?] And my friend was [? Mendel ?] [? Colton, ?] which didn't sound [? odd. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German, the German officer asked him, are you Jew-- in German, are you Jewish? So I jumped. And I told him, no, no, [GERMAN SPEECH], or something like this. Anyway, what I want to say here is, from the beginning, I was already not to be Jewish, to show that we are Polish. Because Jewish is not good to be. The, the German could, could have arrested him or [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, we passed this. What's the translation of the German sentence you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is such a Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '39, July. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, we arrived safely through Germany. Nobody was bothering us. We arrived safely home. And he came to my place, because he lived in another town then. And, and he's living now with his wife in Los Angeles. He can confirm my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here we start the war. Few months later, in, on the first of September, I knew that I can't go back to France. Because it was-- the pa-- the newspapers, and the radio, and the atmosphere was-- everybody knew that something is coming. And in September, they start attacking Poland in-- on Friday morning. And the Polish radio was announcing, here he comes. And here he goes. And here he comes. And he goes. It was irrelevant. It was stupid. This was childish, because in the, the German machine was huge machine. The Poles had a bit of cavalry with untrained artillery and reserves which were never, never trained. It was a bloody disaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: What next? Next, they came to our town, Kalisz. And we had the feeling. First thing, we had the feeling that it's not good. Because they have been beating people and throwing the people in-- and arresting people. And they, they arrested me, too, with some other group of-- in the street. And we were sitting in the city council building, or city town-- town hall, sorry-- in the town hall, in the cellar, for a few hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, they released us. But they took-- they brought me to my father. And they ask my father of the keys from the safe. If not, they won't release me. So, my father gives them the keys of the safe. And in the safe was some-- we had some goods, probably gold and silver, or whatever, nothing of a great importance, maybe. But still, whatever we had, we had in the safe. So they took this. And later on, it was impossible to, to run any business. And the population was hostile as well. So we moved out from the house. And we went to my grandfather's. They had a large [INAUDIBLE] my grandfather, my, my, and my auntie. And the whole family started gathering together. And then I decided to go with my friend Moroco, Tadek Moroco, which I mentioned before, to Warsaw. And we went by train to Warsaw. It was still possible to travel. And very soon, my parents came to Warsaw. And when I left my home-- I mentioned this to you before-- I took only all the pictures, certificates, my matriculation certificates, and things, wrapped in a handkerchief, my sister's handkerchief. I have got it here still. And this is the main thing which I took from home, which is, which was very important. Because any goods, you can replace. And you never can replace dozens of photos which we have here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a very, very clever thing to do. I, I always-- I was always happy about it. And I decided not to throw it away under any circumstances. I go together with the pictures. And I kept them. So I went to Warsaw. And I met-- and my, my parents went to Warsaw as well. We met my parents. And then we decided-- because Poland was divide then by two parts, one part the Russian, one part the Germans. We decided to go over the Bug, the River Bug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe you have seen the picture, Europa. Have you see the picture, Exactly the same story-- we went through the Bug in a boat on a rope, because the, the current was too strong. And once we landed on other side, we were free, the Soviet Union. Nobody asked you about Jewish. Nobody asked you about nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you do when you come to a country which speaks Russian? You study the language. So we enrolled-- until we settled down, we enrolled to a teacher's college in Grodnow-- today Lithuania, I think. And we started studying Russian, which the alphabet is, of course, is completely different. But the words are very similar to Polish. It's not, not such a big, big deal to learn it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, we were not allowed to stay in this town for some reasons, because it was too close to the border. So we went. And it was a very hard. The winter was so harsh. It was incredible. The winter of '39, '40 was terrible. And when, when the spring came, we decided to go down to the Ukraine. The weather is better. The supplies are better. And probably, it's better life or something, whatever. And in the Ukraine, in the town of Kremenets-- we landed in Kremenets-- we enrolled at the teachers' college. Kremenets is a very well-known town in Poland from universities, a kind of small Oxford, something like this, you know, rather a university city with colleges-- different colleges, different schools. And one of the teachers' colleges was a new building, a new established teachers' college. And we decided to enroll. And we were accepted. We were give a scholarship off 180 ruble, which was good enough to keep us going. The teachers' college-- we specialize in physics and mathematics. Many of us had some previous university experience. Some of them were from Manchester, from Lwow University, from Warsaw University. And the one from Manchester lives here in Melbourne. And I met him by chance somewhere. And I didn't know who he was. Then, then we talked together. And then we realized that we went to the same college.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is [? Salig ?] [? Zilberstein. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change the tape.
